From the spectroscopic analysis of 47 GCs, confirmed members of NGC 4382 based on radial velocities, we verified 3 of the 4 photometric groups from the analysis of their stellar populations using the ULySS code. NGC 4382 presents the classic blue ( 10.4 2.8 Gyr, [ Fe/H ] =-1.48 0.18 dex) and red ( 12.1 2.3 Gyr, [ Fe/H ] =-0.64 0.26 dex) GCs formed earlier in the lifetime of the galaxy, and a third group of young GCs ( 2.2 0.9 Gyr; [ Fe/H ] =-0.05 0.28 dex). Finally, analysis of long-slit data of the galaxy reveals a luminosity-weighted mean age for the stellar population of 2.7 Gyr, and an increasing metallicity from [ Fe/H ] = 0.1 to +0.2 dex in R_ gal <10 arcsec (0.87 kpc).

sources.summary: The paper treats M85 as the host of M85 OT 2006-1, citing its NGC 4382 alias, distance, transient association, mean stellar age, faint dust wisps, and blue S0 color.

quotes.quote: Previously published line indices suggest that M85 has a mean stellar age of 1.6 0.3 Gyr.

M85 Optical Transient 2006-1 (M85 OT 2006-1; J122523.82 + 181056.2) was discovered on 2006 Jan 6 by the Lick observatory supernova search team (Filippenko et al. 2001 http://astro.berkeley.edu/ bait/kait.html ) as a faint, V 19.3 mag transient in the galaxy M85 (NGC 4382), which is at a distance of 17.8 Mpc (Mei et al. 2007). Subsequent spectroscopy, as well as visible light and infra-red (IR) photometry, presented in Kulkarni et al. (2007), showed that M85 OT 2006-1 has a recession velocity of 880 130 km s ^ 1 , and is therefore associated with M85.

Ferrarese et al. (2006) reported possible faint wisps and patches of dust in M85. Moreover, Schweizer Seitzer (1992) reported that M85 is somewhat bluer than typical S0 galaxies, therefore possibly younger.

sources.summary: The paper treats 4382 as a merger-remnant bridge case, linking its fine structure, distortions, bluish center, double-peaked nucleus, counterrotating core, and intermediate-age clusters to a past merger.

quotes.quote: 4382 is similar to the merger remnants 7252, 3921, and 7727, but lacks tidal tails. Its rich fine structure and distortions (see Fig. 1 in schweizer88 and Fig. 9 in kormendy09 ) suggest that it experienced a merger 1 - 2 Gyr ago, a hypothesis strengthened by its still bluish center fisher96 , double-peaked nucleus lauer05 , and counter-rotating core mcdermid04 . It was selected specifically to bridge the age gap between ongoing mergers like 4038/39 or 3256 and normal old ellipticals.

The fits, done via Chi-square mapping of the parameter space, yield clear evidence for the presence of intermediate-age clusters in each galaxy. We find that the ages of 1 - 2 Gyr for these globular-cluster subpopulations are consistent with the previously estimated merger ages for the host galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper finds NGC 4382 has a nearly cylindrical outer velocity field, a central twist and decoupled component, clear counter-rotation, a strong dispersion depression, and an h4 ring.

quotes.quote: The velocity field is nearly cylindrical in the outer part. It exhibits a strong twist inwards, with the signature of a decoupled component in the central 2 , where there is also a sign reversal in h_3 BSG94 . A clear counter-rotation is visible from higher resolution OASIS maps mcd04 . The dispersion shows a remarkably strong depression in the central 8--10 , clearly seen in F97 . There is a ring of positive h_4 values at about 8 , hinted in the long-slit data of BSG94 .

sources.summary: The paper reports that NGC 4382 has a previously unreported counter-rotating kinematically decoupled core visible in high-resolution integral-field velocity maps.

quotes.quote: Figure fig:ngc4382_kdc shows an example of how the data can be used to reveal central features of galaxies in the survey. The left panel of this figure presents the velocity field of NGC 4382. There is a low-level `kink' in the zero-velocity contour as it passes through the galaxy centre. The data (right panel) clearly reveal this as a counter-rotating kinematically decoupled component (KDC), which appears almost aligned with the galaxy's main body. This KDC was not previously reported in the literature.

sources.summary: The paper notes that NGC 4382's inner ellipticity disagrees with prior luminosity-weighted values and that its deconvolved ellipticity profile declines inward, with the gradient considered real.

quotes.quote: However, for NGC 4382 and NGC 5813, the inner ellipticity at the core-S ersic break radius disagrees with the Lauer et al. \ (2005) average luminosity weighted ellipticities inside the Nuker break radius. These two galaxies have PSF-deconvolved ellipticity profiles which steadily decrease from 0.5 at R_ b to 0.1 at the galaxies' innermost regions ( R 0 .02 ). While this might be due to the PSF, Rest et al. \ (2001) and Lauer et al. \ (1998; 2005) argued that the deconvolution process can robustly remove the effect of the PSF. These gradients are therefore thought to be real.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 4382 as a young, H-absorption-strong system with a clearly decoupled central component whose KDC was discovered in the authors' observations.

quotes.quote: Similarly, Figure fig:sau_oas_comp shows that NGC 4382 contains a clear decoupled core when observed with .

We find that three of these four galaxies (NGC 3032, NGC 4150, and NGC 4382) with strong H absorption also exhibit a clearly decoupled component in their stellar kinematics. The KDCs in NGC 3032 and NGC 4382 have been discovered from our observations mcdermid05 .

gmw_00000718.name: lensing galaxy in HE 1104 1805
gmw_00000718.overview: The lensing galaxy in HE 1104 1805 is a gravitational lens system located at a redshift of z=0.729 [arXiv:astro-ph/0010403]. Situated in an average density field or small group region [arXiv:1205.4880], the galaxy exhibits detectable Calcium H and K absorption lines [arXiv:astro-ph/0010403].

sources.summary: The paper extracts the faint lens galaxy spectrum in HE 1104 1805, measures z=0.729, detects Calcium H and K absorption, and discusses implications for lens modeling.

quotes.quote: Using FORS2, mounted on Kueyen (UT2 of the VLT), we have obtained the redshift of the lensing galaxy in the gravitational lens system HE 1104 1805 . We measure z=0.729 0.001 , in good agreement with previous estimates based on the time delay and the position of the lens on the fundamental plane. It also coincides with one of the metal line absorption systems that are seen in high resolution spectra of HE 1104 1805 .

The lensing galaxy is four to five magnitudes fainter than component A of HE 1104 1805 and only one arc second away. Even with excellent seeing, the spectrum of the lens is heavily contaminated by that of the quasar and needs to be extracted with sophisticated techniques. The technique implemented by Courbin et al. ( ) spatially deconvolves spectra and decomposes them into point-sources and extended components.

The Calcium H and K absorption lines of the lensing galaxy are clearly detected and the G-band is marginally detected. The redshift was measured by cross correlating the lens spectrum with the elliptical template from the Kinney-Calzetti spectral atlas (Kinney et al. ), which has been plotted (third curve from the bottom) with a shift of 0.05 units in Fig. lens .

sources.summary: The paper treats HE1104 as a robust field/group lens galaxy whose lensing mass exceeds stellar mass enough to require an added cold dark component in MOND/TeVeS.

quotes.quote: However, the other four robust candidates (LBQS1009, HE1104, B1600, HE2149) are located in field/group regions, so that a cold component (CDM) would be required even within the MOND/TeVeS framework. However, the other four galaxies (LBQS1009, HE1104, B1600, HE2149) are located in average density regions (either in the field or in small groups). Hence, for these systems the excess of lensing mass can only be reconciled if an additional, cold, component is added, i.e. CDM.

It was found that, for a range of parameterisations of the MOND and TeVeS theories significant quantities of dark matter are present for at least five galaxies: LBQS1009, HE1104, B1600, HE2149 and Q0957, irrespective of the stellar initial mass function adopted. Although it is noted that Q0957 lives in a high density region -- so that the lensing analysis may be probing scales larger than a typical galaxy halo -- still an additional set of four robust candidates are found (LBQS1009, HE1104, B1600 and HE2149) that pose a serious challenge to these theories.

gmw_00000719.name: NGC 4778
gmw_00000719.overview: NGC 4778 is an S0 lenticular galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0606730, arXiv:astro-ph/0701616] that hosts a low-luminosity active galactic nucleus [arXiv:astro-ph/0701616, arXiv:astro-ph/0701119]. It exhibits a kinematically decoupled, counter-rotating stellar core [arXiv:astro-ph/0606730, arXiv:astro-ph/0701616] and asymmetric outer kinematics that suggest a recent minor merger [arXiv:astro-ph/0606730].

sources.summary: The paper presents NGC4778 as Hickson 62's brightest S0 member, with decoupled nuclear rotation, asymmetric outer kinematics, and evidence for a recent minor merger.

quotes.quote: In this paper we present a kinematical and photometrical study of a member, NGC4778, of the nearest (z=0.0137) compact group: Hickson 62. The aim of this work was to investigate the existence of kinematical anomalies in the brightest group member, NGC4778 in order to constrain the dynamical status and the formation history of the group. We used long-slit spectra obtained with FORS1 at VLT, to measure line-of-sight velocity distributions by means of the Fourier Correlation Quotient method, and to derive the galaxy rotation curve and velocity dispersion profile.

Our analysis reveals that Hickson 62a, also known as NGC4778, is an S0 galaxy with kinematical and morphological peculiarities, both in its central regions ( < 5'' ) and in the outer halo. In the central regions, the rotation curve shows the existence of a kinematically decoupled stellar component, offset with respect to the photometric center. In the outer halo we find an asymmetric rotation curve and a velocity dispersion profile showing a rise on the SW side, in direction of the galaxy NGC4776. The nuclear counterrotation, the distorted kinematics in the outer halo and the X-ray properties of the group suggest that NGC4778 may be the product of a recent minor merger, more reliable with a small late-type galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 4778 as HCG 62’s central S0 galaxy and AGN host, linking X-ray cavities, a cool core, and a counter-rotating core to recent accretion.

quotes.quote: Two deep cavities, due to the presence of the AGN hosted by the central galaxy NGC 4778, are clearly visible in the group X-ray halo. The cavities appear to be surrounded by ridges of cool gas. The group shows a cool core associated with the dominant galaxy.

The group is dominated by the two S0 galaxies NGC 4778 and NGC 4776 with a projected separation of just 8 kpc. HCG 62 is embedded in a bright X-ray halo extending out to >300 kpc with L_X^ bol 10^ 43 erg s ^ 1 . The discovery of two large cavities in its X-ray halo due to the presence of the AGN hosted by the central galaxy NGC 4778 , has recently drawn the attention of the astronomical community on this nearby galaxy group. Furthermore Spavone et al.(2006) have shown that this galaxy has a counter-rotating core and has thus experienced a recent accretion event, indicating a possible link between the AGN activity revealed by the Chandra X-ray observations and the merger event.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 4778 as HCG 62's dominant E3/S0 galaxy and low-luminosity AGN, linking its central X-ray peak, cavities, iron-rich arc, and kinematic peculiarities to AGN activity or a recent merger.

quotes.quote: We find that the diffuse X-ray emission is peaked at the core of the central galaxy NGC 4778, and is dominated by the contribution of the hot gas.

It contains 4 bright member galaxies and is dominated by the E3/S0 galaxy NGC 4778, the brightest member in both optics and X-rays (Pildis et al. \ 1995) that is identified as a low-luminosity AGN (Coziol et al. \ 1998). Kinematic study of the rotation curve and velocity dispersion indicated that NGC 4778 is possibly interacting with another member NGC 4761 (Spavone et al. \ 2006).

We identify a remarkable high-abundance arc region at about 2^ ( 33.6h_ 70 ^ 1 kpc) from the center of NGC 4778, the dominating galaxy of HCG 62. In the arc the average abundance is higher than that in the neighboring regions by a factor of about 2 with the measured metal abundance ratios nicely consistent with the dominance of the SN Ia yields. The arc contains >3 10^ 6 h_ 70 ^ 2.5 \ of iron, or about >3 of the cumulative mass of iron that has been synthesized in the galaxy, which is rather high comparing with the clusters of galaxies that exhibit similar high-abundance structures.

sources.summary: The paper presents NGC4778 as an S0 galaxy in Hickson 62 with central kinematic decoupling and asymmetric outer-halo kinematics toward NGC4776.

quotes.quote: Our analysis reveals that Hickson 62a, also known as NGC4778, is an S0 galaxy with kinematical and morphological peculiarities, both in its central regions ( < 5'' ) and in the outer halo. In the central regions, the rotation curve shows the existence of a kinematically decoupled stellar component, offset with respect to the photometric center. In the outer halo we find an asymmetric rotation curve and a velocity dispersion profile showing a rise on the SW side, in direction of the galaxy NGC4776.

gmw_00000720.name: DF 7
gmw_00000720.overview: DF 7 is an ultra-diffuse galaxy located in the Coma cluster [arXiv:1709.07003, arXiv:2309.15148]. It is characterized by a low surface brightness [arXiv:1709.07003], an old and metal-poor stellar population [arXiv:1709.07003, arXiv:2309.15148], and a phase-space location in the early-infall region of the cluster [arXiv:2309.15148].

sources.summary: The paper presents MaNGA spectra of DF 7, confirming it as a Coma ultra-diffuse galaxy with a low surface brightness, large size, old metal-poor stellar population, and recession velocity near 6599 km/s.

quotes.quote: Here we present optical spectra of three such UDGs, DF 7, DF 44, and DF 17, which have central surface brightnesses of _g 24.4-25.1 mag arcsec ^ 2 . The spectra were acquired as part of an ancillary program within the SDSS-IV MaNGA Survey. We stacked 19 fibers in the central regions from larger integral field units (IFUs) per source. With over 13.5 hr of on-source integration, we achieved a mean signal-to-noise ratio (S/N) in the optical of 9.5 ^ 1 , 7.9 ^ 1 , and 5.0 ^ 1 , respectively, for DF 7, DF 44, and DF 17.

Stellar population models applied to these spectra enable measurements of recession velocities, ages, and metallicities. The recession velocities of DF 7, DF 44, and DF 17 are 6599^ +40 _ 25 , 6402^ +41 _ 39 and 8315^ +43 _ 43 , spectroscopically confirming that all of them reside in the Coma cluster. The stellar populations of these three galaxies are old and metal-poor, with ages of 7.9^ +3.6 _ 2.5 Gyr, 8.9^ +4.3 _ 3.3 Gyr, and 9.1^ +3.9 _ 5.5 Gyr, and iron abundances of [ Fe/H ] 1.0^ +0.3 _ 0.4 , 1.3^ +0.4 _ 0.4 and 0.8^ +0.5 _ 0.5 , respectively.

sources.summary: DF07 is a Coma UDG observed with KCWI, with literature age and metallicity estimates, early-infall phase-space location, GC-like abundance placement, and short-range fitting checks.

quotes.quote: These are the Coma cluster UDGs DFX1 and DF07, along with the Perseus cluster UDG PUDG-R27. DFX1 and DF07 were observed on the nights 2021, April 16th and 17th (Program U105, PI: Brodie) using KCWI with the medium slicer, BL grating and a 4550 central wavelength. These works reported a [ Fe/H ] =--1.33 0.19 dex and an age of 11.2 1.5 for NGC 5846-UDG1 and [ Fe/H ] =--1.03 0.31 dex and an age of 9 1.5 Gyr for DF07.

9 UDGs (PUDG-R27, VCC 1287, Yagi358, Yagi093, Yagi098, Yagi276, Yagi392, Yagi418 and OGS1) are located in the `very early infall' region, with other 2 (DF07 and PUDG-R15) in the `early infall' region. Three UDGs (PUDG-R15, PUDG-R27 and DF07) match the GCs distribution, although in a region that also matches the dEs.

gmw_00000721.name: DF 44
gmw_00000721.overview: Dragonfly 44 is a dispersion-dominated ultra-diffuse galaxy in the Coma cluster [arXiv:1606.06291, arXiv:2311.03876]. It features a massive dark matter halo [arXiv:1606.06291, arXiv:1909.01347], no evidence of rotation [arXiv:1904.04838], and an old, metal-poor stellar population that quenched early [arXiv:2101.02220, arXiv:1709.07003].

sources.summary: The paper presents Dragonfly 44 as a Coma ultra-diffuse galaxy with high velocity dispersion, a dark-matter-dominated inner mass, many globular clusters, and a massive halo.

quotes.quote: Here we present the stellar kinematics of Dragonfly 44, one of the largest Coma UDGs, using a 33.5 hr integration with DEIMOS on the Keck II telescope. We find a velocity dispersion of =47^ +8 _ 6 , which implies a dynamical mass of M_ dyn (<r_ 1/2 ) = 0.7^ +0.3 _ 0.2 10^ 10 M _ within its deprojected half-light radius of r_ 1/2 =4.6 0.2 kpc. The mass-to-light ratio is M/L_I(<r_ 1/2 ) = 48^ +21 _ 14 M _ /L _ , and the dark matter fraction is 98 within r_ 1/2 .

The high mass of Dragonfly 44 is accompanied by a large globular cluster population. From deep Gemini imaging taken in 0 4 seeing we infer that Dragonfly 44 has 94^ +25 _ 20 globular clusters, similar to the counts for other galaxies in this mass range. Our results add to other recent evidence that many UDGs are ``failed'' galaxies, with the sizes, dark matter content, and globular cluster systems of much more luminous objects. We estimate the total dark halo mass of Dragonfly 44 by comparing the amount of dark matter within =4.6 kpc to enclosed mass profiles of NFW halos. The enclosed mass suggests a total mass of 10^ 12 , similar to the mass of the Milky Way.

sources.summary: The paper discusses Dragonfly 44 as a Coma ultra-diffuse galaxy with a rich globular-cluster population, unusually high specific frequency, large stellar-dynamical mass, and an uncertain but near-standard mass ratio.

quotes.quote: Deep imaging has revealed GC populations around two of the Coma UDGs, Dragonfly 44 vandokkum_etal2016 and Dragonfly 17 peng_lim2016 , as well as VCC1287 in Virgo beasley_etal2016 . All three of these systems have anomalously high specific frequencies S_N N_ GC /L_V , but the interest for this discussion is their mass ratio. beasley_etal2016 and vandokkum_etal2016 have already commented that _M for the two UDGs they studied falls close to the standard level applicable for more normal galaxies.

For Dragonfly 44, vandokkum_etal2016 find a relatively rich GC population N_ GC 94 25 and, again, a specific frequency S_N higher by an order of magnitude than normal galaxies with similar L_V . With M_ GC = 1.1 10^5 M_ for the galaxy luminosity M_V^T = -16.1 , then = (1.0 0.3) 10^7 M_ . Their measurement of the velocity dispersion of the stellar light gives M(<r_ 1/2 ) = 7.1 10^9 M_ . The total (virial) mass M_h is likely to be at least an order of magnitude higher, but scaling from the comparable results for VCC1287 above see beasley_etal2016 , we obtain a very rough estimate M_h 2 10^ 11 M_ . In turn, the final result is _M 5.5 10^ 5 , again very uncertain.

sources.summary: The paper treats Dragonfly 44 as a massive UDG useful for dark-matter tests and presents KCWI kinematics showing no rotation, a rising dispersion profile, and a cored-halo explanation for the data.

quotes.quote: In this context UDGs such as VCC 1287 ( Beasley et al. 2016), Dragonfly 17 ( Peng Lim 2016), and Dragonfly 44 ( van Dokkum et al. 2016) occupy an interesting region of parameter space, as their stellar masses are a factor of 100 higher than those of dwarf spheroidal galaxies. If they lie on or above the canonical halo mass -- stellar mass relation ( Moster , Naab , White 2013) their halos masses are also much higher than those of dwarf spheroidals, and they could help disentangle the processes shaping the distribution of dark matter on kpc scales.

We present spatially-resolved stellar kinematics of the well-studied ultra diffuse galaxy (UDG) , as determined from 25.3 hrs of observations with the Keck Cosmic Web Imager. The luminosity-weighted dispersion within the half-light radius is _ 1/2 = 33^ +3 _ 3 , lower than what we had inferred before from a DEIMOS spectrum in the H region. There is no evidence for rotation, with V_ max / <0.12 (90 confidence) along the major axis, in possible conflict with models where UDGs are the high-spin tail of the normal dwarf galaxy distribution.

\ has a rising velocity dispersion profile, from =26^ +4 _ 4 \ at R=0.2 kpc to =41^ +8 _ 8 \ at R=5.1 kpc. The profile can only be fit with a cuspy NFW profile if the orbital distribution has strong tangential anisotropy, with =-0.8^ +0.4 _ 0.5 . An alternative explanation is that the dark matter profile has a core: a Di Cintio et al. \ (2014) density profile with a mass-dependent core provides a very good fit to the kinematics for a halo mass of (M_ 200 / M _ )=11.2^ +0.6 _ 0.6 and = -0.1^ +0.2 _ 0.3 , i.e. \ isotropic orbits.

sources.summary: The paper models DF44 as a Coma ultra-diffuse galaxy with resolved kinematics, high velocity dispersion, MOND fits in isolation, and possible distance, disruption, or baryonic-halo explanations.

quotes.quote: notably coined the term ultra-diffuse galaxies when they uncovered its large population of low surface brightness objects with the Dragonfly Telephoto Array Abraham2014 , and the first spatially resolved kinematics of a UDG was obtained for a Coma cluster UDG: DF44 vanDokkum2019b . DF44 The velocity and velocity dispersion profiles of DF44 were obtained with Keck Cosmic Web Imager KCWI, Morrissey2012, Morrissey2018 , an integral field unit (IFU) spectrograph on the Keck II telescope optimised for low surface brightness objects. These profiles were studied in the context of Newtonian gravity assuming CDM by , in the context of MOND by and . further considered Newtonian gravity with fuzzy dark matter, an exotic form of DM cf.

These two studies used the VanDokkum2015, vanDokkum2016,VanDokkum2017,vanDokkum2019b I band luminosity L_I=3.0 0.6 10^8 L_ and projected half-light radius R_e=4.7 kpc of DF44 to infer the gravitational acceleration within MOND and, from it, the line-of-sight velocity dispersion profile through Jeans modelling. assumed a spherical S ersic profile given the projected S ersic index n=0.94 , fixed the stellar mass-to-light ratio to 1.3, and considered different anisotropy parameters. assumed a spherical Plummer profile and left both the dynamical mass-to-light ratio and the anisotropy parameter free. interpreted the agreement between the velocity dispersion profile with the MOND prediction in isolation as an indication that DF44 is either far from the cluster centre (the projected distance is 1.8 Mpc), undergoing disruption, or embedded in a dark baryonic halo.

sources.summary: The paper presents MaNGA spectra of DF 44, confirming it as a Coma ultra-diffuse galaxy with old metal-poor stars, velocity near 6402 km/s, and unusually high dispersion and globular-cluster evidence.

quotes.quote: Here we present optical spectra of three such UDGs, DF 7, DF 44, and DF 17, which have central surface brightnesses of _g 24.4-25.1 mag arcsec ^ 2 . The spectra were acquired as part of an ancillary program within the SDSS-IV MaNGA Survey. We stacked 19 fibers in the central regions from larger integral field units (IFUs) per source. With over 13.5 hr of on-source integration, we achieved a mean signal-to-noise ratio (S/N) in the optical of 9.5 ^ 1 , 7.9 ^ 1 , and 5.0 ^ 1 , respectively, for DF 7, DF 44, and DF 17.

Stellar population models applied to these spectra enable measurements of recession velocities, ages, and metallicities. The recession velocities of DF 7, DF 44, and DF 17 are 6599^ +40 _ 25 , 6402^ +41 _ 39 and 8315^ +43 _ 43 , spectroscopically confirming that all of them reside in the Coma cluster. The stellar populations of these three galaxies are old and metal-poor, with ages of 7.9^ +3.6 _ 2.5 Gyr, 8.9^ +4.3 _ 3.3 Gyr, and 9.1^ +3.9 _ 5.5 Gyr, and iron abundances of [ Fe/H ] 1.0^ +0.3 _ 0.4 , 1.3^ +0.4 _ 0.4 and 0.8^ +0.5 _ 0.5 , respectively.

sources.summary: The paper targets Dragonfly 44 with UV and X-ray data, finding quiescent UV limits, no X-ray detection, no hot corona, and LMXB limits consistent with low stellar mass.

quotes.quote: The Dragonfly 44 UV luminosity is also consistent with quiescence, with SFR <6 10^ 4 M_ yr ^ 1 , and no X-rays are detected down to a sensitivity of 10^ 38 erg s ^ 1 . This rules out a hot corona with a M > 10^8 M_ within the virial radius, which would normally be expected for a dynamically massive galaxy. The absence of bright, low mass X-ray binaries is consistent with the expectation from the galaxy total stellar mass, but it is unlikely if most low-mass X-ray binaries form in globular clusters, as Dragonfly 44 has a very large population.

One Coma UDG, DF44, has received particular attention because its large number of GCs and stellar velocity dispersion imply a Milky Way-like dynamical mass of 10^ 12 M_ , whereas its stellar mass of 3 10^8 M_ is consistent with a dwarf galaxy vdokkum2015,vdokkum2016,vdokkum2017,gu2018 . This implies an extreme dark matter fraction exceeding 99 , which challenges traditional galaxy models, especially as DF44 may be representative of larger UDGs.

The only galaxy with relatively tight limits on LMXB emission is DF44, and the limit of 10^ 38 erg s ^ 1 is fully consistent with the expectation from its low stellar mass. However, if most LMXBs are formed in GCs, the lack of detection in DF44 is surprising.

sources.summary: DF44 is described as a Coma UDG with extended kinematics, a preferred cored dark-matter halo, high halo mass, and adopted rich globular-cluster count.

quotes.quote: DF44 As noted in the Introduction, DF44, in the Coma cluster, is the only UDG to have published radially extended kinematics and the only one to have an independent dynamical measure of its total halo mass. From deep exposures on the Keck II telescope using KCWI, measured a velocity dispersion profile out to 5 kpc with no sign of bulk rotation. This data was well fit by a cored dark matter halo which indicated isotropic orbits giving a total halo mass of log M _h = 11.2 0.6. Isotropic orbits were preferred from the shape of the absorption lines. A cuspy NFW fit to the data required strongly tangential orbits and gave a lower total halo mass of log M _h = 10.6 ^ +0.4 _ 0.3 .
The published studies of GC counts for DF44 range from 20 ^ +6 _ 5 from S22 to 76 18 from vD17. However, as discussed above, we have elected to adopt the vD17 GC number.

sources.summary: The paper uses DF44 as a spectroscopic UDG benchmark, estimating a 43 km s^-1 dispersion consistent with the published value and concluding it sits at the upper end of UDG size and enclosed mass.

quotes.quote: We evaluate _v^* for the two UDGs with published velocity dispersions VCC 1287, from six globular cluster velocities, and DF 44, from integrated light; respectively beasleya,vdk16 . For VCC 1287 we obtain a velocity dispersion of 24 km s ^ 1 in comparison to the published value of 33 ^ +16 _ 10 km s ^ 1 , while for DF44 we obtain 43 km s ^ 1 in comparison to the published value of 47 ^ +8 _ 6 km s ^ 1 . Our estimates are within 1 of the published results.

DF44 appears to lie in a massive ( 10^ 12 M_ ) as found by , but is not representative of UDGs. It lies at the upper end in size and enclosed mass of the known UDGs. Therefore, while it appears to be what has been referred to as a failed L _* galaxy, it is not typical of UDGs.

sources.summary: The paper uses DF44 as the observed Coma UDG benchmark, noting its measured velocity dispersion, high dark-to-stellar mass ratio, and tension with the EMOND/fundamental-manifold prediction.

quotes.quote: The effective radius was determined and corrected for ellipticity for 46 UDGs within the Coma cluster The sample actually has 47 objects, but one object has incomplete data in the table and thus we disregard this entry. and are given in . Currently, there is only one UDGs (Dragonfly 44 (DF44)) in the Coma cluster which has a measured value for the velocity dispersion. Therefore, to estimate velocity dispersions for all 46 galaxies in the Coma cluster sample, some assumptions have to be made.

If we take the estimate for DF44, which is the only UDG in the sample which has been observed ( 47^ +8 _ 6 km/s), the FM under-predicts the velocity dispersion by a factor of 2.7 (FM predicted velocity dispersion for DF44 is 17.4 km/s). If we then take the published observed data from , EMOND would predict a Newtonian mass of 7.7 10^ 8 M _ and thus the ratio between this EMOND (q=1 model) predicted Newtonian mass and the stellar mass is 7.5 which is quite a substantial difference.

More detailed observations of more UDGs in the Coma cluster will be required to determine whether the over-massive dark halo of DF44 is a statistical outlier in the sample, or whether the interpretation of the FM used in our work is at a contention with the current observations.

sources.summary: The paper uses DF44 as an extreme ultra-diffuse galaxy, citing its large effective radius, high inferred halo mass, and mild outlier status relative to the sample.

quotes.quote: On the other hand, estimated a virial mass m 10^ 12 from the stellar velocity dispersion and globular cluster count of DF44, a particularly large UDG with =4.5 kpc . Such mass is comparable to that of the Milky Way, suggesting that at the high-mass end UDGs may be as massive as L^* galaxies.

With the exception of DF44, all UDGs have measured masses smaller than our upper limit, as expected. f:shmr30kpc shows that DF44 is a mild outlier in terms of halo mass compared to its stellar mass. While the stellar mass of DF44, 3 10^8 , is similar to the average stellar mass in our sample, it has an effective radius =4.5 kpc , significantly larger than the average of our sample. In fact, DF44 is among the largest UDGs from a sample of more than 800 discovered in Coma koda15 , so it is not entirely surprising that it may be an outlier in the halo mass-stellar mass plane, and suggests that---as with any galaxy sample---there is a large scatter in the halo masses of UDGs.

sources.summary: The paper uses Dragonfly 44 as an ultra-diffuse local galaxy with extreme inferred dark matter dominance, finding its reported mass-to-light ratio and luminosity scaling consistent with MONDian expectations.

quotes.quote: For the recently studied Dragonfly 44 and Crater II systems , reported (M/L)_ N values are also in good agreement with MONDian expectations.

To these 8 dSph we have added the recently discovered Dragonfly 44 and Crater II systems, well measured ultra diffuse local galaxies which appear as two of the most heavily dark matter dominated systems known in their luminosity ranges, van Dokkum et al. (2015), van Dokkum et al. (2016) and Caldwell et al. (2017) . The observed parameters for these systems and relevant references are summarised in table 1, where all observed and inferred luminosities throughout our study are quoted always in the V band. Total Newtonian masses for these 10 objects where then derived using eq. (1), which when divided by the observed luminosities yields (M/L)_ N . This quantity yields the y-coordinate of the points shown in figure (1), with the corresponding error bars resulting from the error propagation of the reported observational uncertainties in , R_ e and L for these galaxies.

Notice again the consistency of the much brighter ultradiffuse Dragonfly 44 system, the inclusion of which allows to extend the comparison beyond the narrow range over which the ultrafaint dSphs occur, to actually allow for a meaningful power law fit to the data.

sources.summary: The paper uses Dragonfly 44 as a massive observed UDG benchmark, arguing its velocity dispersion, globular clusters, and central mass can be explained by early quenching rather than an over-massive halo.

quotes.quote: This hypothesis is supported by the stellar velocity dispersion and the number of globular clusters (GCs) of a massive UDG, Dragonfly 44, in the Coma cluster for which vanD16,vanD17 inferred a total halo mass 10^ 12 .

They can only match the absolute magnitude and effective radius of Dragonfly 44 for t_ q 2 Gyr , i.e. z_ q 3.3 . At those times, their halo masses were around 10^ 11 and their inner masses matched Dragonfly 44. Hence, Dragonfly 44 can form in a halo similar to the MW's progenitor, but it has to be quenched very early. This means that at the time of quenching, Dragonfly 44 is not hosted by a highly "over-massive" DM halo. Its central halo mass, which appears unusually high for its estimated stellar mass, can be explained by its early formation time.

sources.summary: The paper compares wave-dark-matter predictions with DF44, modeling its flat velocity-dispersion profile using a soliton core and interpreting its mass as dwarf-like.

quotes.quote: Currently the best studied example, DF44, has a uniform velocity dispersion of 33 km/s, extending to at least 3 kpc, that we show is reproduced by our DM simulations with a soliton radius of 0.5 kpc. In the DM context, we show the relatively flat dispersion profile of DF44 lies between massive galaxies with compact dense solitons, as may be present in the Milky Way on a scale of 100pc and lower mass galaxies where the velocity dispersion declines centrally within a wide, low density soliton, like Antlia II, of radius 3 kpc.

We now apply the above to the newly measured dispersion profile of the recently discovered galaxy ``Dragon Fly 44" (DF44). This unusual galaxy was recently discovered in a Coma cluster survey using the unique multi-beam optical Dragon Fly imager, designed to search for extended low-surface-brightness emission Dokkum2016 . This unique telescope has successfully surveyed sizeable areas to unprecedentedly low levels of surface brightness from the ground Abraham2014 , uncovering a surprising, unknown extreme class of ``Ultra Diffuse Galaxies", that are smooth, diffuse clouds of stars found mainly in massive galaxy clusters, that contrast with the centrally sharp and bright elliptical galaxy members Dokkum2016 . The galaxy DF44 is one of the largest example of this UDG class, with a half-light radius 4.6 kpc, and a stellar mass of 3 10^ 8 M_ . Its stellar velocity dispersion profile has recently been measured with deep spectroscopy Dokkum2016,Dokkum2019 .

sources.summary: The paper constrains Dragonfly 44 with Chandra and XMM-Newton non-detections, arguing its X-ray limits rule out a massive failed-L galaxy halo and favor a dwarf-like halo.

quotes.quote: In this work, I present Chandra and XMM-Newton observations of two archetypal UDGs, Dragonfly 44 and DF X1, and I constrain their dark matter halo mass based on the X-ray emission originating from hot gaseous emission and from the population of low-mass X-ray binaries (LMXBs) residing in GCs. Both Dragonfly 44 and DF X1 remain undetected in X-rays. The upper limits on the X-ray emission exclude the possibility that these galaxies reside in massive ( M_ vir 5 10^ 11 \ M_ ) dark matter halos, suggesting that they are not failed L_ galaxies. These results demonstrate that even these iconic UDGs resemble to dwarf galaxies with M_ vir 10^ 11 \ M_ , implying that UDGs represent a single galaxy population.

The most likely candidate UDGs with massive dark matter halos are Dragonfly 44 (hereafter DF 44) and DF X1, which galaxies were studied in extensive follow-up campaigns. Deep optical observations suggest the existence of a substantial GC population around these galaxies, which is indicative of massive dark matter halos 2016ApJ...828L...6V,2017ApJ...844L..11V,2019ApJ...880...91V .

sources.summary: The paper measures Dragonfly 44's stellar populations, finding low metallicity, flat age and metallicity gradients, globular-cluster-like central abundances, and evidence for rapid inside-out formation followed by early quenching.

quotes.quote: We use the Keck Cosmic Web Imager integral-field unit spectrograph to: 1) measure the global stellar population parameters for the ultra-diffuse galaxy (UDG) Dragonfly 44 (DF44) to much higher precision than previously possible for any UDG, and 2) for the first time measure spatially-resolved stellar population parameters of a UDG. We find that DF44 falls below the mass--metallicity relation established by canonical dwarf galaxies both in and beyond the Local Group. We measure a flat radial age gradient ( m_ age +0.01_ 0.08 ^ +0.07 log Gyr kpc ^ 1 ) and a flat-to-positive metallicity gradient ( m_ Fe/H +0.08_ 0.11 ^ +0.11 dex kpc ^ 1 ), which are inconsistent with the gradients measured in similarly pressure-supported dwarf galaxies.

We also measure a flat-to-negative [ Mg/Fe ] gradient ( m_ Mg/Fe 0.18_ 0.17 ^ +0.17 dex kpc ^ 1 ) such that the central 1.5 kpc of DF44 has stellar population parameters comparable to metal-poor globular clusters. Overall, DF44 does not have internal properties similar to other dwarf galaxies and is inconsistent with it having been puffed up through a prolonged, bursty star-formation history, as suggested by some simulations. Rather, the evidence indicates that DF44 experienced an intense epoch of ``inside-out'' star formation and then quenched early and catastrophically, such that star-formation was cut off more quickly than in canonical dwarf galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper discusses DF44 as an extraordinary ultra-diffuse galaxy whose revised mass, halo scale, and high mass-to-light ratio motivate searches for similarly large UDGs.

quotes.quote: For example, higher fidelity stellar kinematic measurements and globular cluster counts now result in estimates of (M/L)_I 26 M_ /L_ within the effective radius for DF44 vanDokkum2019b and a total mass in the neighborhood of 10 ^ 11 M _ rather than the original estimates that placed it closer to 10 ^ 12 M _ vanDokkum2019b, Saifollahi2020 . Even so, DF44 and other similarly sized UDGs are extraordinary. Compare them with another galaxy of total mass 10 ^ 11 M _ , the Large Magellanic Cloud erkal . The mass-to-light ratio within the half-light radius of the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) is only 4.6 (calculated using r_ 1/2 from Drlica-Wagner2020 , the enclosed i-band luminosity from Eskew2011 , and the HI rotation curve of Kim1998 ) in comparison to 26^ +7 _ 6 for DF44 in solar units vanDokkum2019b .

For example, DF44, (circularized r_e = 3.9 kpc), has an estimated halo mass of 10^ 11 M _ vanDokkum2019b,Saifollahi2020 . These physically large systems are highlighted in Figures fig:zplot and fig:phase_space with bold, black outlined markers.

sources.summary: The paper treats DragonFly 44 as a dispersion-dominated ultra-diffuse galaxy with low stellar mass, dominant dark matter, and fractional-gravity fits favoring large s.

quotes.quote: To these we add the dispersion-dominated ultra-diffuse galaxy DragonFly 44 . Note that ultra-diffuse galaxies are a mixed bag of objects with very different properties: some feature large angular momentum, a rich gas reservoir with ongoing active star-formation activity ; others show no signs of rotation, a poor gas content and old stellar population in passive evolution . DragonFly 44 belongs to this last category, and being dispersion-dominated has been treated here along with the dwSph sample. In addition, it is a particularly interesting object since it features a very small stellar mass with respect to its large size; in fact, the DM mass is expected to dominate even at small radii. Therefore DragonFly 44 has been exploited as an useful laboratory to test the nature of DM and gravity .

Finally, in the cases of Fornax and DragonFly 44 there is a clear preference for large values of s 1.5 , but the improvement in the FG fits with respect to the Newtonian case, albeit clear at a visual inspection, is not statistically significant enough to make definite conclusions.

sources.summary: DF44 is described as a well-studied Coma UDG with measured gradients, debated rich globular-cluster counts, and possible recent infall as part of a small group.

quotes.quote: DF44 DF44 is in the Coma cluster and has been one of the best-studied UDGs to date. It is one of only two UDGs that has had spatially resolved kinematic and stellar population gradients measured (the other being NGC 1052-DF2). This interest has mostly been the result of claims of a rich GC system associated with the galaxy vanDokkum2017 although there is currently some disagreement on the total GC numbers of DF44 in the literature Saifollahi2021, Saifollahi2022 .

While we classify DF44 as being in the Coma cluster, its phase space positioning suggests it may just be beginning to infall as part of a small group vanDokkum2019b . As such, some authors have classified it with low-density UDGs when considering its formation (e.g., FerreMateu2023 ). The radial velocity was derived using V_ = (1+z) from the redshift listed in footnote 6 of z= 0.02132 vanDokkum2017 .

sources.summary: The paper uses DF 44 as a benchmark Coma UDG, citing its measured velocity dispersion, massive dark halo interpretation, large effective radius, and very low stellar content.

quotes.quote: In the case of one Coma UDG, DF 44, a stellar velocity dispersion was subsequently measured vdk16 . The velocity dispersion is indeed consistent with that expected if that galaxy lies in a massive dark matter halo, but the measurement is taken at small radius relative to the calculated virial radius and so weakly constrains the total halo mass. Furthermore, the exposure time required to obtain this measurement was extreme and analogous measurements now exist for only a couple of other UDGs beasleya,vdk17 .

The largest confirmed UDGs, those with existing spectroscopic redshifts, are DF08 with r_e = 4.4 kpc and DF44 with r_e = 4.6 kpc vdk15a,vdk17,kadowaki17 and there are a few others that are slightly smaller but still have r_e > 4 kpc kadowaki17 .
DF44, for example, is estimated to have a total mass close to that of the Milky Way and yet only contain 1 as many stars vdk15b .

sources.summary: The paper models Dragonfly 44 in MOND, finding good central agreement for an isolated UDG and arguing it must be far from the Coma core or possibly disrupted.

quotes.quote: Using Jeans modelling, we calculate the velocity dispersion profile of the ultra-diffuse galaxy (UDG) Dragonfly 44 in MOND. For the nominal mass-to-light ratio from the literature and an isotropic profile, the agreement with the data is excellent near the center of the galaxy. However, in modified gravity, close to the cluster core, the gravitational environment should bring the galaxy back towards the Newtonian behavior. This success of the isolated MOND prediction for the central velocity dispersion could then mean that the galaxy is at large distances ( 5 Mpc) from the cluster core, as hinted by the fact that close-by UDGs share similar velocities with a dispersion well below that of the cluster itself. There is however a two-sigma tension in the outer part of the UDG due to an increase of the observed dispersion profile with respect to the flat MOND prediction. This deviation could simply be a measurement error. Other possibilities could be, for a UDG far from the cluster, a higher-than-nominal baryonic mass with a tangentially anisotropic dispersion profile, or even a sign of a dark baryonic halo. If the UDG is closer to the cluster core, the deviation could be a sign that it is in the process of disruption.

sources.summary: The paper highlights DF44 as a well-studied Coma UDG with the richest GC system in the sample, consistent halo-mass estimates, and a massive halo suggesting a failed galaxy.

quotes.quote: The UDG with the highest GC number (76) is DF44 2017ApJ...844L..11V . Perhaps the best studied Coma UDG is that of DF44. As shown in 2019arXiv190100900B , its halo mass based on radially extended kinematics and its halo mass based on its GC count are in excellent agreement.
The highest S _N galaxy is DF44. This UDG has been well-studied and has a massive halo of 10 ^ 11 M _ 2019ApJ...880...91V , making it a good candidate for a failed galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper discusses DF44 as a Coma UDG with a narrow star-formation history, flat gradients, debated globular-cluster counts, and possible early feedback-driven quenching.

quotes.quote: Villaume21 studied DF44, a UDG in the outskirts of the Coma cluster, and determined that the galaxy has a very narrow SFH and a flat radial stellar metallicity and age gradient. They conclude that this is consistent with DF44 having formed entirely, along with its large population of 100 GCs This estimate has been challenged by Saifollahi21 and Bogdan20 . Saifollahi21 estimate a much lower number, 20 GCs, in DF44. vanDokkum16_DF44 , in a single burst of star formation 10 ago. Since it is likely that DF44 was quenched before falling into Coma, this raises the possibility that the same SF burst that produced the large population of GCs and expanded the stars also quenched the galaxy 10 ago. In this scenario, DF44 formed near the mean SMHM relation vanDokkum19_DF44 and had an early strong burst of SF that expanded the stellar component to =4.6 , flattening the age and metallicity gradients, and quenching the galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper uses DF44 as an iconic UDG benchmark, comparing revised globular-cluster counts with simulated UDGs and noting possible absence of analogs under earlier estimates.

quotes.quote: The iconic UDG DF44 is a good example vanDokkum2016 . Originally thought to host nearly 100 GCs vanDokkum2016 , it has been now estimated to have only 20 GCs Saifollahi2021 . If we take the latest measurements as correct, our simulated UDGs are a good representation of galaxies like DF44. On the other hand, if earlier estimates are found to hold, then we do not find DF44 analogs in our sample. The example set by DF44 perhaps warrants a closer look into observed galaxies with very extreme GC content.

sources.summary: DF44 is placed among mixed-timescale UDGs, has low magnesium enhancement despite early-infall location, and is grouped with GC-rich early-forming candidates suggestive of massive halos.

quotes.quote: 4 UDGs are located in the `mixed times' regions (PUDG-S74, PUDG-R84, DFX1, DF44) all presenting quenching timescales of 6--8 Gyr. DF44 instead presents a relatively low [ Mg/Fe ] Villaume2022 even though it is located in the `early infall' region. These are Yagi358, PUDG-R84, DF44 and DGSAT I.

UDGs that are located in the `late-infall' region of Figure fig:phasespace_fig or that are known to be in an infalling group, such as DF44, are shown in all panels. From those, PUDG-R84 and Yagi358 (in high-density environments), and DF44 (as infall galaxy), present early and fast formation and are GC-rich, indicative of a large dark matter halo.

sources.summary: The paper treats DF44 as the benchmark large Coma UDG: it is spectroscopically confirmed, quiescent, has prominent absorption features, and supports the inference that similar Dragonfly UDGs are Coma members.

quotes.quote: Of the population discovered in proximity to the Coma Cluster, DF44 ( = 13 00 58.0 ; = 26 58 35 ) is the only UDG spectroscopically confirmed as a Coma member vanDokkum2015b . For example, noted that the spectrum of DF44 is similar to that of early-type galaxies, with Balmer and G-band absorption lines, concluding that DF44 is a quiescent galaxy with no significant on-going or recent star formation.

Four of our UDGs (DF07, DF08, DF30, and DF40), plus the previously measured DF44, fall within the caustics and we conclude these are bona fide members. Coupled with the spectroscopic confirmation of DF44 vanDokkum2015b , the result that 5 of 6 spectroscopically observed, physically large (projected half light radius > 2.9 kpc) UDGs from the catalog are bona fide cluster members suggests that 15 of the 18 similarly large Dragonfly UDGs are Coma members.

sources.summary: DF44 is described as a Coma ultra-diffuse galaxy with many globular clusters, high GC-system mass, and an estimated halo mass making it an outlier.

quotes.quote: The third UDG is DF44 in the Coma cluster 2016ApJ...828L...6V . It has a stellar mass of 3 10^8 and 74 18 GCs 2017ApJ...844L..11V , which corresponds to a GC system mass of approximately 7.4 10^6 . These three UDGs all have very high GC system mass to galaxy stellar mass ratios approaching 10 .
Based on its stellar velocity dispersion, DF44 has an estimated halo mass of 8 10^ 11 2016ApJ...828L...6V and is an outlier in the plot.

sources.summary: Dragonfly 44 is one of two Coma ultra-diffuse galaxies used to test MRF; after compact-source subtraction, the UDGs become the brightest objects and are readily identifiable.

quotes.quote: We focus on a 12 4 22 9 section that contains two UDGs, Dragonfly 44 and DFX1 (see van Dokkum et al. 2017). The galaxies are not easily distinguished from other sources in the field, which is why van Dokkum et al. (2015) used a complex multi-stage process to eliminate faint objects from an initial candidate list of 6624. The right panel shows the same region after applying the mrf code, with default parameters. The two UDGs are now the two brightest objects in this region, and they can readily be identified using standard image detection software.

sources.summary: The paper uses DF 44 as a real-data uncertainty test, comparing Keck and Binospec velocity-dispersion precision and reproducing a reported 7 km/s uncertainty with its formula.

quotes.quote: Our velocity dispersion estimates from intermediate-resolution ( R 4,800 ) spectra having low signal-to-noise ratio of 4--5 per pixel were questioned by colleagues, in particular by comparing our uncertainties (7--9 km s ^ 1 ) to those (7 km s ^ 1 ) obtained by 2016ApJ...828L...6V for a similar ultra-diffuse galaxy DF 44 using a larger 10-m Keck telescope and much longer integration time (33.5 h vs 2 h) that yielded a substantially higher signal-to-noise ratio of 14 per pixel. The key difference was a spectral configuration and a wavelength range used in the two studies. We used a blue optical setup covering a lot of prominent absorption lines in a wide wavelength range (3900 < < 5300 , same as used in the Monte-Carlo simulations presented earlier), while 2016ApJ...828L...6V used a narrow spectral region centered on H (6444 < < 6679 ), which contains very few absorption lines. We applied Eq. eqerr_simple to a stellar population template having age of 10 Gyr and [ Fe/H ] = 1.5 dex representative of a spectrum of DF 44 with all the parameters reported in the paper ( , spectral resolution, sampling, etc.) and obtained an estimated uncertainty of 6.5 km s ^ 1 fully consistent with 7 km s ^ 1 presented by 2016ApJ...828L...6V .

sources.summary: The paper uses DF44 as a GC-rich UDG comparison object whose high dynamical mass agrees with simulations and whose resolved stellar kinematics show no evidence for rotation.

quotes.quote: The GC-rich UDGs with the highest masses (DFX1, DF44) have dynamical masses consistent with simulated galaxies over the full redshift range.

To date, only two UDGs have spatially-resolved stellar kinematic profiles: NGC1052-DF2 Emsellem_et_al_19 and DF44 van_Dokkum_et_al_19 . Both galaxies have rich GC systems 3-4 per cent of the galaxy luminosity, van_Dokkum_et_al_18b, van_Dokkum_et_al_19 and thus, based on the results of this work, we would not expect them to be rotationally-supported galaxies.
DF44 shows no evidence for rotation V/ < 0.12 van_Dokkum_et_al_19 , but has an inferred dynamical mass ( M_ dyn 4 10^9 ) that is consistent with the simulated galaxies (Figure  fig:Mdyn ).

sources.summary: Dragonfly 44 is a large Coma UDG with high velocity dispersion and an unusually rich globular cluster population; the paper confirms its Coma membership and DFX1-like color.

quotes.quote: The galaxies, Dragonfly 44 and DFX1, have effective radii of 4.7 kpc and 3.5 kpc and velocity dispersions of 47^ +8 _ 6 \ and 30^ +7 _ 7 , respectively.
Both galaxies are associated with a striking number of compact objects, tentatively identified as globular clusters: N_ gc =74 18 for Dragonfly 44 and N_ gc =62 17 for DFX1.

DFX1 and Dragonfly 42 contribute to the steadily growing sample of UDGs with confirmed distances (see Kadowaki , Zaritsky , Donnerstein 2017), and our redshifts confirm that Dragonfly 44, DFX1, and Dragonfly 42 are all members of the Coma cluster.

sources.summary: DF44 is discussed as a high-velocity-dispersion UDG whose stellar and globular-cluster dispersions imply a massive dark halo and agree with simulated born-UDGs.

quotes.quote: Halo masses estimates are necessarily indirect, but it has been argued that the high stellar velocity dispersions of systems like DF44 and DFX1 indicate halos as massive as 10^ 12 M_ , suggestive of ``failed'' Milky-Way like galaxies vanDokkum2017 .

Published studies report a wide range of velocity dispersions for UDGs, from _ los 4 km/s in objects like DF2 and DF4 so low that it may imply little to no dark matter, vanDokkum2018a,vanDokkum2019,Danieli2019 to as high as 50 km/s for the globular cluster population ( 33 km/s for the stellar dispersion at R_ eff ) of DF44, which suggests a fairly massive dark matter halo vanDokkum2016, vanDokkum2019_DF44 .
High-velocity dispersion UDGs, such as DF44, are in agreement with simulated B-UDGs.

gmw_00000722.name: DF 17
gmw_00000722.overview: DF 17 is an ultra-diffuse galaxy located within the Coma cluster [arXiv:1709.07003, arXiv:1701.04845]. It features a low central surface brightness [arXiv:1709.07003], an old and metal-poor stellar population [arXiv:1709.07003], and a population of globular clusters [arXiv:1701.04845].

sources.summary: The paper presents MaNGA spectra of DF 17, confirming it as a Coma ultra-diffuse galaxy with low surface brightness, large size, old metal-poor stars, and the first recession velocity measurement near 8315 km/s.

quotes.quote: Here we present optical spectra of three such UDGs, DF 7, DF 44, and DF 17, which have central surface brightnesses of _g 24.4-25.1 mag arcsec ^ 2 . The spectra were acquired as part of an ancillary program within the SDSS-IV MaNGA Survey. We stacked 19 fibers in the central regions from larger integral field units (IFUs) per source. With over 13.5 hr of on-source integration, we achieved a mean signal-to-noise ratio (S/N) in the optical of 9.5 ^ 1 , 7.9 ^ 1 , and 5.0 ^ 1 , respectively, for DF 7, DF 44, and DF 17.

Stellar population models applied to these spectra enable measurements of recession velocities, ages, and metallicities. The recession velocities of DF 7, DF 44, and DF 17 are 6599^ +40 _ 25 , 6402^ +41 _ 39 and 8315^ +43 _ 43 , spectroscopically confirming that all of them reside in the Coma cluster. The stellar populations of these three galaxies are old and metal-poor, with ages of 7.9^ +3.6 _ 2.5 Gyr, 8.9^ +4.3 _ 3.3 Gyr, and 9.1^ +3.9 _ 5.5 Gyr, and iron abundances of [ Fe/H ] 1.0^ +0.3 _ 0.4 , 1.3^ +0.4 _ 0.4 and 0.8^ +0.5 _ 0.5 , respectively.

sources.summary: The paper presents Dragonfly 17 as a Coma ultra-diffuse galaxy with a measured globular-cluster population and a halo mass inferred consistently from the authors' calibration and previous work.

quotes.quote: Deep imaging has revealed GC populations around two of the Coma UDGs, Dragonfly 44 vandokkum_etal2016 and Dragonfly 17 peng_lim2016 , as well as VCC1287 in Virgo beasley_etal2016 . All three of these systems have anomalously high specific frequencies S_N N_ GC /L_V , but the interest for this discussion is their mass ratio.

Dragonfly 17: For this UDG in Coma, peng_lim2016 estimate a total population N_ GC = 28 14 based on a well sampled GCLF and radial distribution. For M_V^T = -15.1 , we have M_ GC = 0.94 10^5 M_ and then predict a total mass M_ h = (9.1 4.5) 10^ 10 M_ for this galaxy. peng_lim2016 predicted M_ h = (9.3 4.7) 10^ 10 M_ , although the agreement with our estimate is something of a coincidence: they used a higher value of from an older calibration, but also a higher mean GC mass, and these two differences cancelled out.

gmw_00000723.name: NGC 2903
gmw_00000723.overview: NGC 2903 is a relatively isolated barred spiral galaxy [arXiv:2206.13311, arXiv:2306.09854] that hosts a central starburst [arXiv:1209.5443, arXiv:astro-ph/0610114] and a nuclear ring-like structure [arXiv:0709.1236]. The galaxy features a young stellar bar that drives a strong inflow of dense molecular gas toward its center [arXiv:0809.4177].

sources.summary: The paper maps dense molecular gas in NGC 2903, finding bar-enhanced emission, central star formation, long bar consumption times, and a young inflowing bar.

quotes.quote: University of Ja en, Ja en, Spain Instituto de Astrof sica de Andaluc a-CSIC, Granada, Spain University of Toronto, Astronomy Department, Toronto, Canada Received XX; accepted XX We have mapped the emission from two spiral galaxies, NGC 2903 and NGC 3504 to study the gas properties in the bars. The emission is detected in the center and along the bar of NGC 2903. The line ratio / ranges from 0.07 to 0.12 with the lowest value in the center. The enhancement of emission along the bar indicates a higher fraction of dense molecular gas in the bar than at the center. The mass of dense molecular gas in the center ( 2.2 10^7 ) is about 6 times lower than that in the bar ( 1.2 10^8 ).

Comparison of the distribution of in the bars of NGC 3504 and NGC 2903 with the results from the numerical simulations of Martin Friedli (1997) suggest that the bars of these galaxies are at different stages of evolution. The bar in NGC 3504 is of type C with age 1 Gyr, with most of the gas already in its center. NGC 2903 harbors a young type B bar with an age between 200 and 600 Myr, with a strong inflow of gas toward the center.

sources.summary: The paper maps CO emission across NGC 2903, deriving regional molecular gas densities and temperatures and linking spatial SFE variations to molecular gas density.

quotes.quote: We present simultaneous mappings of J=1-0 emission of ^ 12 CO, ^ 13 CO, and C ^ 18 O molecules toward the whole disk ( 8' 5' or 20.8 kpc 13.0 kpc) of the nearby barred spiral galaxy NGC 2903 with the Nobeyama Radio Observatory 45-m telescope at an effective angular resolution of 20'' (or 870 pc). We detected ^ 12 CO( J=1-0 ) emission over the disk of NGC 2903. In addition, significant ^ 13 CO( J=1-0 ) emission was found at the center and bar-ends, whereas we could not detect any significant C ^ 18 O( J=1-0 ) emission. In order to improve the signal-to-noise ratio of CO emission and to obtain accurate line ratios of ^ 12 CO( J=2-1 )/ ^ 12 CO( J=1-0 ) ( R_ 2-1/1-0 ) and ^ 13 CO( J=1-0 )/ ^ 12 CO( J=1-0 ) ( R_ 13/12 ), we performed the stacking analysis for our ^ 12 CO( J=1-0 ), ^ 13 CO( J=1-0 ), and archival ^ 12 CO( J=2-1 ) spectra with velocity-axis alignment in nine representative regions of NGC 2903.

We found that both R_ 2-1/1-0 and R_ 13/12 differ according to the regions, which reflects the difference in the physical properties of molecular gas; i.e., density ( n_ H_2 ) and kinetic temperature ( T_ K ). We determined n_ H_2 and T_ K using R_ 2-1/1-0 and R_ 13/12 based on the large velocity gradient approximation. The derived n_ H_2 ranges from 1000 cm ^ 3 (in the bar, bar-ends, and spiral arms) to 3700 cm ^ 3 (at the center) and the derived T_ K ranges from 10 K (in the bar and spiral arms) to 30 K (at the center). We examined the dependence of star formation efficiencies (SFEs) on n_ H_2 and T_ K , and found the positive correlation between SFE and n_ H_2 with the correlation coefficient for the least-square power-law fit R^2 of 0.50.

sources.summary: The paper lists NGC 2903 as a partial Milky Way twin: its optical radius and rotation curve are close, but its stellar mass and oxygen abundance distribution differ appreciably.

quotes.quote: The galaxy NGC 2903 is an Sbc spiral galaxy (morphological type code T = 4.0 0.1). Its inclination angle is i = 65 , and the position angle of the major axis is PA = 204 deBlok2008 . The optical radius of NGC 2903 is R_ 25 = 5.87 arcmin Walter2008 . The distance to NGC 2903 is d = 8.9 Mpc Drozdovsky2000 , and it results in a physical optical radius of R_ 25 = 15.21 kpc. The stellar mass of NGC 2903 based on the mean value from Jarrett2019 and Leroy2021 and rescaled to the adopted distance is M_ = 3.33 10 ^ 10 M_ , or log( M_ /M_ ) = 10.52.

The values of the optical radii of NGC 2903 ( R_ 25 = 15.21 kpc) and the Milky Way ( R_ 25 = 12.0 kpc) are close to each other, and the rotation curves (panel (b) of Fig. figure:ngc2903--oh ) in those galaxies agree satisfactorily to each other. However, the value of the stellar mass of NGC 2903 (log( M_ /M_ ) = 10.52) is appreciably lower than the stellar mass of the Milky Way (log( M_ /M_ ) = 10.716). There is also an appreciable difference between the radial distributions of the oxygen abundances (panel (a) of Fig. figure:ngc2903--oh ) in the galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 2903 as an early barred hot-spot spiral with a nuclear ring, stellar clusters, active circumnuclear star formation, and substantial molecular gas in its inner kiloparsec.

quotes.quote: NGC 2903 is a well studied galaxy. The Paschen image obtained with the Hubble Space Telescope (HST) reveals the presence of a nuclear ring-like morphology with an apparent diameter of approximately 15 = 625 pc 2001MNRAS.322..757A . This structure is also seen, though less prominent, in the H observations from . A large number of stellar clusters are identified on high resolution infrared images in the K' and H bands, which do not coincide spatially with the bright HII regions.

A possible interpretation of this is that the stellar clusters are the result of the evolution of giant HII regions e.g. 2001MNRAS.322..757A . The global star formation rates in the nuclear ring, as derived from its H luminosity is found to be 0.1 M _ yr ^ 1 by and 0.7 M _ yr ^ 1 from 2001MNRAS.322..757A . From CO emission observations, derive a mass of molecular gas (H _2 ) of 1.8 10 ^ 8 M _ inside a circle 1 Kpc in diameter.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes NGC 2903 as a strongly barred galaxy, showing that H data reveal pronounced non-axisymmetric flow more clearly than the lower-resolution H I map.

quotes.quote: However, the data from NGC 2903 are good enough over almost the entire barred region ( R 60 ) to yield velocities above our S/N threshold. In this galaxy, \ estimate the median value of m=2 streaming motions in the bar region is only 14 km s ^ 1 , and conclude that the mean elongation of the overall potential is nevertheless consistent with being round. The application of \ to this galaxy presents a substantial challenge because the bar is so closely aligned with the major axis of projection. As discussed in section better , the velocities , V_ 2,t V_ 2, become harder to distinguish as | _b| 0 , and \ can return unphysically large values for all ( 1000 km s ^ 1 in magnitude), even though they still fit the data well when combined as eq. ( pveleq ).

However, the H data show a much more pronounced non-circular flow pattern within the bar region ( R 60 ), with perturbed velocities almost as large as , which in turn significantly exceeds the estimated circular speed from an axisymmetric fit to the same data (black line) and the derived \ from the HI data. The fitted velocities from the two datasets are in reasonable agreement outside the bar region, for as far as the optical data extend. The uncertainties in the bar region are still large, and significantly larger than the point to point variation in the best fit suggesting that slightly more aggressive smoothing could be warranted.

sources.summary: The paper presents NGC 2903 as a nearby barred spiral with central starburst activity, bar star formation, complex multiwavelength morphology, and patchy UV regions arranged nearly perpendicular to the bar.

quotes.quote: NGC 2903 is a nearby barred spiral with an active starburst in the center and \ regions distributed along its bar. We aim to analyse the star formation properties in the bar region of NGC 2903 and study the links with the typical bar morphological features. A combination of space and ground--based data from the far--ultraviolet to the sub--millimeter spectral ranges is used to create a panchromatic view of the NGC 2903 bar.

NGC 2903 is a complex galaxy, with a very different morphology on each spectral band. The CO ( J =1-0) and the 3.6  \ emission trace each other in a clear barred structure, while the leads both components and it has an s--shape distribution. The UV emission is patchy and does not resemble a bar. The UV emission is also characterised by a number of regions located symmetrically with respect to the galaxy center, almost perpendicular to the bar, in a spiral shape covering the inner 2.5 kpc.

sources.summary: For NGC 2903, the paper averages Hubble and brightest-star distances, uses K87 photometry, builds the rotation curve from H-alpha and outer data, and notes THINGS reaches 25 kpc.

quotes.quote: NGC 2903 The Hubble distance to this galaxy is 6.3 Mpc, while a distance estimate based on the brightest stars (Drozdovsky Karachentsev 2000) is 8.9 1.9 Mpc. Presently we take as distance the average of the two: 7.6 Mpc. Photometry is measured by K87 giving the profile and total light. The rotation curve is composed of H emission line data by Marcelin et al. (1983) for the inner 100 \ and data by Begeman (1987) beyond, out to 29 kpc.
The H radial velocity measurements have been transformed into a rotation curve using the same inclination of 62 as for the kinematics. The THINGS rotation curve goes out to 25 kpc.

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 2903 as an exception whose unusual H I profile and gas distribution prevent the chemical-evolution model from reproducing its metallicity profile well.

quotes.quote: The fits of the remaining galaxies are shown in Fig.  figure12 to  figure14 . For all these galaxies, except NGC 2903, the fits of the observed metallicity distributions are reasonable within the uncertainties of the observations and the range of galactocentric distances where observations are available. But for most of these objects the ^2 isocontour diagrams indicate that the values of and are not very well constrained by the fit. We add these objects to the plot of the location of galaxies in the ( , ) plane in Fig.  figure15 and find that we still can identify three well distinguished groups in our sample, in the same way as already discussed above. The galaxy NGC 2903 has an unusual H i profile with a steep decline of gas mass from the centre to 0.5r/R_ 25 and then an extended disk with constant gas mass. Our models are unable to reproduce chemical profiles for such gas distribution properly.
The exceptions are NGC 4736 and 2903 with a steeper decline.

sources.summary: The paper uses NGC 2903 as a representative barred spiral, finding enhanced inner noncircular molecular gas motions and a bar-aligned velocity field that traces its pattern speed.

quotes.quote: Examples of the results of calculations of v_ , , v_r , and for two representative galaxies of SA (NGC 5055) and SB (NGC 2903) types are shown in figure fig:ex , while the rest of the sample is plotted in Appendix. Figure fig:ex shows several important features of these systems: (1) The kinematics is clearly dominated by circular rotation at all radii ( v_r v_ ), especially outside the bar region, and the derived v_ of both galaxies are consistent with the shapes and amplitudes of the rotation curves derived from HI data dB08 . (2) The fraction of noncircular motions is somewhat larger in the inner region of the barred galaxy; within R<50 , we find 0.15 in NGC 5055 and 0.20 in NGC 2903.

(3) Panel () shows a nearly linear rise of v_ in NGC 2903 up to the radius R 4  kpc , resembling the rotation of a rigid body. This is a consequence of the fact that the bar position angle of this galaxy is nearly the same as the position angle of the host galaxy (the difference is 3 ; see table tab:gal1 ). As discussed in section D13 , the observed circular velocity in this case traces the rotation of the bar itself and reveals its angular velocity, which appears to be nearly constant within 30 <R<90 .

sources.summary: NGC 2903 is described as an isolated, nearly face-on barred galaxy with nuclear hot spots, recent star formation, and significant HI and H2 along the bar co-rotation radius.

quotes.quote: NGC 2903 ( (J2000) = 09:32:10.1 and (J2000) = + 21:30:03) is a barred galaxy that could be hosting a young bar with an age between 200-600 Myr with a strong inflow of gas towards the centre along the bar Leon_2008 . The galaxy is seen nearly face-on, as shown in Figure figure:fig1 . The galaxy is non-interacting and isolated Irwin_2009 , with many intense emission regions (“hot spots”) in the nuclear region along with a ring of star formation Perez_2000 .

The star formation progression and HI/H _ 2 distribution of NGC 2903 differs from that of the other three galaxies studied here. Although a prominent stellar bar is detected in Spitzer 3.6 imaging data, NGC 2903 shows recent star formation (100-300 Myr) and significant HI and H _2 along the bar co-rotation radius. This galaxy is located in an isolated environment and is an ideal case to examine the secular evolution due to bar-induced processes.

sources.summary: The paper derives center estimates and harmonic-decomposition results for NGC 2903, describing its disk morphology, non-circular motions, and whether the inferred potential is consistent with being round.

quotes.quote: NGC 2903 NGC 2903 (Fig. fig:ngc-2903 ) a) Center estimates [ 1.5ex ] NGC 2903 is a barred galaxy with tightly wound spiral arms. Its dominant bar can be seen both in the IRAC image and in the radio continuum map. The radio continuum additionally shows a strong nuclear source, coinciding within 1 \ with the center of the bulge in the 3.6 m image. The central position as given by the radio continuum ( = 1 2, =2 4 ) was used to make a \ run with all parameters left free.

sources.summary: The paper derives stellar and gas velocity dispersions for NGC 2903's nucleus and circumnuclear star-forming regions, using HST sizes to estimate dynamical masses of embedded stellar clusters.

quotes.quote: Gas and star velocity dispersions have been derived for four circumnuclear star-forming regions (CNSFRs) and the nucleus of the spiral galaxy NGC 2903 using high resolution spectroscopy in the blue and far red. Stellar velocity dispersions have been obtained from the CaII triplet (CaT) lines at 8494, 8542, 8662 , using cross-correlation techniques while gas velocity dispersions have been measured by Gaussian fits to the H 4861 \ line. The CNSFRs, with sizes of about 100 to 150 pc in diameter, show a complex structure at the Hubble Space Telescope (HST) resolution, with a good number of subclusters with linear diameters between 3 and 8 pc. Their stellar velocity dispersions range from 39 to 67 km s ^ 1 . These values, together with the sizes measured on archival HST images yield upper limits to the dynamical masses for the individual star clusters between 1.8 and 8.7 10 ^6 M _ and upper limits to the masses for the whole CNSFR between 4.9 10 ^6 and 4.3 10 ^7 M _ .

sources.summary: The paper finds bright polarized arcs on both sides of NGC 2903's minor axis, increasing polarized fraction with radius, and magnetic vectors roughly following optical spiral structures.

quotes.quote: NGC 2903 In this galaxy, one of the Starburst supplement to the basic SINGS sample, bright polarized arcs are detected along both sides of the minor axis trailing away in brightness slowly to the northeast and more rapidly to the southwest. The lowest brightness of polarized emission occurs on the receding major axis (PA = 204 ^ , as tabulated in Table table:summary ). The polarized fraction increases from about 1 in the inner parts to about 5--15 at intermediate radii, to as high as 40 . The magnetic field vectors are roughly parallel to optical spiral arm structures for the minor axis features, although generally with a slightly larger radial component. Field lines run almost perpendicular to the linear major axis feature.

sources.summary: The paper uses NGC 2903 as a high-surface-brightness spiral test case, comparing its observed rotation curve with the authors' nonlocal nonlinear gravity formalism and MOND.

quotes.quote: To illustrate the practical applicability of the formalism developed here, we construct the rotation curves of two standard high- and low- surface brightness galaxies and compare the results with those obtained through MOND's formalism. NGC 2903 is a textbook example of a high surface brightness spiral. It has a large stellar component and small HI content. The gas is confined to the galactic plane and follows circular orbits. It is well observed out to about 40 kpc ( ).

sources.summary: The paper places this THINGS galaxy in Group B, where BDM fits outperform NFW, imply r_c roughly comparable to r_s, and favor a strong dark-matter core.

quotes.quote: In the second case, Group B, (IC 2574 (B1), NGC 2366 (B2), NGC 2903 (B3), NGC 2976 (B4), NGC 3198 (B5), NGC 3521 (B6), and NGC 925 (B7)), we get a fitted value of r_c r_s and BDM profile fits better than NFW. In fact, the NFW profile in some cases over-predicts the velocity in the inner parts of the galaxy or does fit with unphysical values for r_s > O (10^4) or _0 < O (10^3) M_ /kpc^ 3 , see Table tab:onlydm_bdm and tab:onlydm_nfw . The BDM profile gives r_c r_s for all stellar mass models, having an average r_c value of 6 and 5 kpc for Min.Disk and Min.Disk+Gas, respectively. Since r_c r_s , implying that our BDM profile is of the type (r_c + )^ 3 and hence having a strong core behavior. For this set of galaxies the Burkert and ISO profiles, as well as the BDM, fit much better than NFW, indicating that a core is needed.

sources.summary: The paper says NGC 2903 requires more than twice the predicted M/L for a satisfactory MOND fit and remains problematic unless that high M/L can be explained.

quotes.quote: NGC 2903 The contour plots in Fig fig:com2903 show that in order to have a satisfactory fit, regardless of distance and stellar scale-height, the M/L must be more than twice the predicted value. If this is the case, then an excellent fit can be achieved as shown in the top left panel. On the other hand, using a M/L of only 50 larger than the predicted value gives a poor fit, as the dotted line attests. Therefore, NGC 2903 is a problem for MOND unless a reason can be found why it should have a M/L that is twice the diet-Salpeter prediction. If such a reason was found, it would be well fit by MOND.

sources.summary: The paper presents deep observations of NGC 2903, finding soft diffuse nuclear and extended emission, a brighter central starburst zone, and a hard thermal wind-fluid component linked to massive-star feedback.

quotes.quote: We present a deep \ observation of the central regions of the late-type barred spiral galaxy NGC 2903. The \ data reveal soft ( kT_e 0.2--0.5 keV) diffuse emission in the nuclear starburst region and extending 2 \ ( 5 kpc) to the north and west of the nucleus. Much of this soft hot gas is likely to be from local active star-forming regions; however, besides the nuclear region, the morphology of hot gas does not strongly correlate with the bar or other known sites of active star formation. The central 650 pc radius starburst zone exhibits much higher surface brightness diffuse emission than the surrounding regions and a harder spectral component in addition to a soft component similar to the surrounding zones. We interpret the hard component as being also of thermal origin with kT_e 3.6 keV and to be directly associated with a wind fluid produced by supernovae and massive star winds similar to the hard diffuse emission seen in the starburst galaxy M82.

sources.summary: The paper models NGC 2903 outside its bar-affected inner region, using an NFW halo and a two-component stellar distribution while avoiding bar-streaming corrections beyond the paper's scope.

quotes.quote: NGC 2903 The results of the mass modelling for NGC 2903 are plotted in Figure fig:N2903-NFW . An NFW halo model was fitted to the outer regions using the THINGS H i observed rotation curve, as described in the Appendix. NGC 2903 hosts a strong bar and the rotation curve in the inner 3 for the H i and CO are strongly affected by streaming motions. The stellar mass surface density is described using a 2-component decomposition. We do not use the rotation curve corrected for the bar-streaming motions derived in Section sec:N2903-df .

sources.summary: The paper confirms two NGC 2903 satellite candidates, treating UGC 5086 as reliable and dw0930+2143 as confirmed using redshift plus consistent SBF distance.

quotes.quote: NGC 2903 Table tab:ngc2903_sbf lists the SBF results for NGC 2903. We confirm two candidates as satellites and consider two more as possible/unconfirmed satellites. Our SBF distance of UGC 5086 is trustworthy given the smooth, round morphology of that galaxy. The other confirmed satellite, dw0930+2143, is bluer, more irregular, and H I rich irwin2009 so the SBF distance is less certain. irwin2009 measured a redshift for this dwarf via H I observations that is close to that of NGC 2903 ( cz 30 km/s).

sources.summary: The paper models NGC 2903 with an inner bar flow, finding small bar velocities, similar results to earlier work, and a possible ring-like residual.

quotes.quote: NGC 2903 Since the 3.6 m Spitzer image shows the disc of this galaxy to be barred, we have included a bar flow to the fitted model in the inner part of the disc. The fitted radial and azimuthal bar flow velocities SS07 are indicated, respectively, by the green and blue points with error bars. The bar flow velocities are small compared with the mean orbital speed, which agrees well with that estimated by dB08 who found, not surprisingly, that axisymmetric fits in the bar region required the PA and inclination to vary. SZS10 also used an earlier version of \ and included a bar flow but to 60 \ only. We find similar results as they did when we limit the non-asisymmetric part to the same region, but here allow for a somewhat more extensive bar flow.

sources.summary: The paper uses NGC 2903 as the low-agreement case study, describing it as an isolated spiral with low NED-SIMBAD classification match fraction driven by region-versus-association interpretations.

quotes.quote: NGC 2903 is a large, relatively isolated spiral galaxy irwin2009 at a distance of 8.89 Mpc, with a luminosity L_K = 10^ 10.9 L _ kara13 . This galaxy has a notably low total match fraction in comparison to other galaxies within the LVG: only about 35 of the NED and SIMBAD objects compared within NGC 2903 were determined to have the same classification. fig:CS2_Object_Distribution illustrates the comparison of object classifications between NED and SIMBAD for NGC 2903.
